content:
# Exodus 40 -- Disagreement Log

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

## Summary

Exodus 40 -- the final chapter of Exodus -- narrates YHWH's command to erect the tabernacle (vv. 1-16), Moses's obedient execution (vv. 17-33), and the climactic descent of the glory-cloud upon the completed sanctuary (vv. 34-38). The chapter is among the most formulaic in Exodus: the execution narrative (vv. 17-33) repeats the instruction narrative (vv. 1-8) almost verbatim, and the refrain "as YHWH commanded Moses" appears eight times. This high degree of internal repetition produces strong agreement across all three translators. The most consequential disagreements concern "veil" vs. "curtain" (parokhet), "dwelt" vs. "settled" vs. "had settled" (shakhan), "taken up" vs. "went up" vs. "lifted" (he'alot), and "holy of holies" vs. "most holy" (qodesh qodashim). The theophany in vv. 34-38, despite being the theological climax, generated only minor divergences because all three translators recognized the weight of the language and rendered it conservatively.

**Agreement rate:** ~95% verbatim or near-verbatim across the three translations.

---

## Theologically Significant Disagreements

### 1. Vv. 3, 21, 22, 26 -- "Veil" vs. "curtain" vs. "shielding curtain" (parokhet)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "curtain" |
| LOGOS | "veil" |
| PNEUMA | "curtain" (v. 3), "shielding curtain" (v. 21) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"veil"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew parokhet denotes the inner screen separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. The outer entrance hangings are designated masakh ("screen"). EMET renders both parokhet and masakh with near-synonyms ("curtain" and "screen"), collapsing a distinction the Hebrew maintains. PNEUMA compounds parokhet as "shielding curtain" in v. 21, adding an interpretive modifier. LOGOS consistently renders parokhet as "veil," preserving its unique theological identity as the barrier concealing the divine presence from the holy place.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Veil" for parokhet has been SOPHIA's consistent rendering throughout the tabernacle narrative (Exodus 26:31-35; 27:21; 30:6; 35:12; 36:35; 38:27; 39:34; 40:3, 21, 22, 26). The veil is theologically distinct from the screen (masakh): it conceals the ark and the divine presence, and it is the barrier that defines the Most Holy Place. Using "curtain" for both parokhet and masakh would obscure this structural-theological distinction. PNEUMA's "shielding" adds a modifier not in the Hebrew; the protective function is already inherent in the verb sakakh ("to screen") in v. 3 and need not be duplicated in the noun.

---

### 2. V. 10 -- "A holy of holies" vs. "most holy" (qodesh qodashim)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "a holy-of-holies" |
| LOGOS | "most holy" |
| PNEUMA | "most holy" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"a holy of holies"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew qodesh qodashim is a superlative construct -- literally "a holy of holies" or "a holiness of holinesses." It is applied here to the altar of burnt offering upon its anointing: the altar becomes so consecrated that anything touching it participates in the highest degree of sanctity. LOGOS and PNEUMA render the superlative idiomatically as "most holy," which is smooth English but flattens the distinctive Hebrew construction. EMET preserves the construct with hyphenation ("a holy-of-holies").

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "A holy of holies" preserves the Hebrew superlative construct without hyphenation. This phrase is theologically loaded: it is the same construction that names the inner sanctum (the Holy of Holies). By rendering the construct transparently, the reader perceives the connection between the altar's status and the innermost chamber -- both bear the same designation of maximal sanctity. "Most holy" is an English superlative that obscures this structural connection. The hyphenation in EMET is unnecessary; the phrase reads naturally without it. This is consistent with SOPHIA's rendering in Exodus 29:37, 30:10, 30:29, and 30:36.

---

### 3. V. 35 -- "Dwelt" vs. "settled" vs. "had settled" (shakhan)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "dwelt" |
| LOGOS | "dwelt" |
| PNEUMA | "had settled" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"dwelt"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew shakhan is the verbal root of mishkan ("tabernacle/dwelling-place"). In v. 35, the cloud shakhan ("dwelt/tabernacled") upon the tent of meeting -- a profound theological wordplay: the mishkan is the place where YHWH yishkon ("dwells"). EMET and LOGOS both render "dwelt," preserving this connection. PNEUMA renders "had settled," which captures the aspect of coming to rest but loses the etymological resonance between shakhan and mishkan. The pluperfect "had settled" also shifts the temporal frame, suggesting the settling preceded the filling -- an interpretation not required by the Hebrew, which presents shakhan and male' as simultaneous descriptions of the same theophany.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Dwelt" follows the EMET/LOGOS majority and preserves the shakhan-mishkan wordplay, which is the single most important etymological connection in the tabernacle theology. The cloud "dwelt" upon the "dwelling-place" -- the English echo of "dwelt" and "dwelling" (used for mishkan in many passages) maintains a relationship that "settled" would sever. The LXX's epeskiazen ("overshadowed") introduces a different nuance entirely, but SOPHIA follows the MT.

---

### 4. Vv. 36-37 -- "Was taken up" vs. "went up" vs. "lifted" (he'alot / ye'aleh)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "went up" (v. 36), "did not go up" (v. 37) |
| LOGOS | "was taken up" (v. 36), "was not taken up" (v. 37) |
| PNEUMA | "lifted" (v. 36), "did not lift" (v. 37) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"was taken up"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew be-he'alot (v. 36) is a Niphal infinitive construct of 'alah ("to go up"), and ye'aleh (v. 37) is a Niphal imperfect of the same root. The Niphal stem indicates passive or reflexive action: the cloud "is caused to go up" or "is taken up" -- it does not ascend of its own initiative but is lifted by divine agency. EMET renders with the active "went up," losing the passive voice of the Niphal. PNEUMA's "lifted" captures the passive sense but loses the directional component of 'alah ("upward"). LOGOS's "was taken up" preserves both the passive voice and the upward direction.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Was taken up" follows LOGOS because it faithfully renders the Niphal passive while preserving the directional meaning of 'alah. The passive is theologically significant: the cloud does not "go up" on its own, nor do the Israelites "lift" it -- it is taken up by YHWH's sovereign action, signaling the time for departure. EMET's active rendering obscures this divine agency. PNEUMA's "lifted" is intransitive, which could suggest the cloud lifts itself. "Was taken up" unambiguously conveys passive action from an external agent.

---

## Moderate Disagreements

### 5. Vv. 4, 25 -- "Set up" vs. "caused to go up" vs. "lit" (ve-ha'aleta / va-ya'al)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "you shall cause to go up" (v. 4), "he caused to go up" (v. 25) |
| LOGOS | "set up" (v. 4), "set up" (v. 25) |
| PNEUMA | "set up" (v. 4), "set up" (v. 25) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"set up"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew ha'alah (Hiphil of 'alah) when used of lamps literally means "to cause to go up" -- referring to the raising of the flame. This is the same Hiphil root used for offering sacrifices ("to cause to go up on the altar"). EMET preserves the literal Hiphil semantics transparently ("cause to go up"). LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "set up," which is standard English for kindling or preparing lamps.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Set up" was chosen because "cause to go up" is opaque in English as a lamp-related action. The reader would not understand that "causing lamps to go up" means kindling them. "Set up" conveys the action naturally. The verbal connection to 'alah is noted in the disagreement but does not justify obscuring the meaning for the English reader. The LXX's epitheseis ("you shall place upon") likewise renders the functional sense rather than the etymological root.

---

### 6. V. 2 -- "Raise up" vs. "erect" vs. "set up" (taqim)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "you shall raise up" |
| LOGOS | "you shall erect" |
| PNEUMA | "you are to set up" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"you shall raise up"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew taqim is the Hiphil imperfect of qum ("to rise/stand"). The Hiphil means "to cause to stand, to raise up." EMET preserves the directional Hiphil sense ("raise up"). LOGOS renders the functional equivalent ("erect"). PNEUMA uses the informal "set up." The verb taqim/huqam is the pivotal verb of the chapter -- it appears in vv. 2, 17, 18 (twice), and 33, forming the structural backbone of the narrative: YHWH commands Moses to "raise up" the tabernacle, and Moses "raises it up."

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Raise up" follows EMET because it preserves the Hiphil causative of qum and maintains verbal consistency across all five occurrences in the chapter. "Erect" (LOGOS) is a functional synonym but loses the directional upward sense. "Set up" (PNEUMA) is too generic -- one "sets up" a card table. The tabernacle is raised up -- it rises from the ground through Moses's hands, an act that echoes the language of resurrection (qum) and establishment. The repeated use of this verb throughout vv. 17-33 creates a rhythmic motif that "raise up" preserves.

---

### 7. V. 20 -- "Mercy seat" vs. "atonement cover" (kapporet)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "mercy-seat" |
| LOGOS | "mercy seat" |
| PNEUMA | "mercy seat" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"mercy seat"** |

**Discussion:** All three translators agree on "mercy seat" (with minor hyphenation differences in EMET). The Hebrew kapporet derives from the root k-p-r ("to atone/cover"). The LXX renders it as hilasterion ("place of propitiation"), the same word Paul applies to Christ in Romans 3:25. No disagreement in substance; SOPHIA standardizes without hyphenation.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** Full consensus. "Mercy seat" without hyphen, consistent with SOPHIA's established practice.

---

### 8. V. 33 -- "Finished" vs. "completed" (va-yekhal)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "finished" |
| LOGOS | "finished" |
| PNEUMA | "finished" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"finished"** |

**Discussion:** All three translators render va-yekhal (Piel of kalah) as "finished." SOPHIA notes that in Exodus 39:32, the same root was rendered "completed" to preserve the Genesis 2:1-2 creation echo (va-yekhullu). Here in 40:33, "finished" is equally valid -- the Piel va-yekhal emphasizes Moses as the active agent who brings the work to conclusion, whereas 39:32 used the Niphal/Qal passive to describe the work being completed. The distinction is between "the work was completed" (39:32, passive, echoing "the heavens and earth were completed") and "Moses finished the work" (40:33, active, echoing "God finished his work" in Genesis 2:2). Both verbs are from kalah, but the English "finished" better suits the active construction here.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** Full consensus on "finished." The creation-echo is maintained because Genesis 2:2 itself uses the active construction ("God finished his work"), and English "finished" is the natural rendering of the active Piel. The parallel is: God finished (va-yekhal) his work // Moses finished (va-yekhal) the work. The verbal match holds in both Hebrew and English.

---

### 9. V. 15 -- "Everlasting" vs. "eternal" vs. "permanent" (olam)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "eternal" |
| LOGOS | "everlasting" |
| PNEUMA | "permanent" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"everlasting"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew olam denotes an indefinitely long duration -- "for the age," "perpetual," "everlasting." EMET's "eternal" carries metaphysical connotations of timelessness that may exceed the Hebrew sense (olam is durational, not atemporal). PNEUMA's "permanent" is institutional language that lacks the sacral register the context demands (priesthood established by divine anointing). LOGOS's "everlasting" occupies the middle ground: it conveys long, open-ended duration with a formal, elevated register appropriate to covenant language.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Everlasting" follows LOGOS. It preserves the durational sense of olam without the metaphysical absoluteness of "eternal," and it maintains the sacral register that "permanent" lacks. The phrase "everlasting priesthood" has a long pedigree in English Bible translation and is immediately recognizable. This is consistent with SOPHIA's rendering of olam in covenant contexts throughout the translation.

---

### 10. V. 29 -- "Offered up" vs. "offered" (va-ya'al)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "offered up" |
| LOGOS | "offered up" |
| PNEUMA | "offered" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"offered up"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew va-ya'al (Hiphil of 'alah) literally means "caused to go up" -- the same verb used for kindling lamps (v. 25). In sacrificial contexts, it denotes the offering ascending as smoke to YHWH. PNEUMA drops the directional "up," rendering simply "offered." EMET and LOGOS preserve the upward direction.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Offered up" follows EMET/LOGOS. The upward direction is integral to the theology of the olah (burnt offering), whose very name derives from 'alah ("to go up"). The offering ascends -- it goes up to YHWH. Dropping "up" severs the lexical connection between the verb (he-'elah, "he caused to go up") and the noun (olah, "that which goes up"). In v. 29, both the verb and noun share the same root, and "offered up... the burnt offering" preserves this resonance.

---

### 11. Vv. 36-38 -- "Set out" vs. "journey" (yis'u)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "would journey" |
| LOGOS | "would set out" |
| PNEUMA | "would set out" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"would set out"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew yis'u (from nasa', "to pull up stakes, to decamp, to set out") denotes the act of breaking camp and beginning a march. EMET's "journey" describes the ongoing travel rather than the initial departure. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "set out," which captures the moment of departure -- the pulling up of tent pegs and beginning of movement.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Would set out" follows the LOGOS/PNEUMA majority. The root nasa' specifically denotes the act of decamping -- the initial departure, not the journey itself. The cloud's lifting signals the moment to break camp and begin marching, and "set out" captures this inchoative sense. The imperfect tense ("would set out") correctly renders the iterative/habitual aspect: this was the repeated pattern throughout the wilderness period.

---

### 12. V. 38 -- "Would be" vs. "was" vs. "glowed" (tihyeh)

| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "would be" |
| LOGOS | "was" |
| PNEUMA | "glowed" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"would be"** |

**Discussion:** The Hebrew tihyeh is an imperfect form of hayah ("to be"), expressing habitual or iterative action: "fire would be in it by night." LOGOS renders with the simple past "was," which could imply a single occasion. PNEUMA renders "glowed," adding a vivid visual element not present in the Hebrew verb hayah, which means only "to be/exist." EMET preserves the imperfect as "would be," correctly indicating the repeated, ongoing nature of the fire's nightly appearance.

**SOPHIA's reasoning:** "Would be" follows EMET. The imperfect tihyeh expresses what happened repeatedly throughout Israel's journeys -- every night, fire would be in the cloud. "Was" (LOGOS) flattens the iterative aspect into a single past event. "Glowed" (PNEUMA) substitutes a visually evocative English verb for the plain Hebrew "be," adding aesthetic content not present in the source text. The Hebrew is simple and majestic in its simplicity: fire would be in it by night. SOPHIA preserves that plainness.

---

## LXX Divergences Noted

All three translators flagged the following LXX divergences. SOPHIA follows MT in each case:

1. **Chapter numbering:** The Vaticanus LXX (p. 83, heading "CAP. XXXIX" visible at top) numbers this material differently from the MT in some traditions. The LXX chapter 39 contains material that in the MT falls under chapter 40. Despite this numbering divergence, the content of the erection commands, execution narrative, and theophany align closely between MT and LXX for this chapter.

2. **LXX plus material in vv. 17ff:** Codex Vaticanus (pp. 83-84) contains an extensive plus section within the execution narrative, repeating fabrication details from the earlier construction chapters (corresponding to MT chs. 36-39). This is the well-known LXX Exodus tabernacle divergence: the Greek tradition interleaves construction details with the erection account, while the MT presents them as separate blocks. SOPHIA follows the MT's more economical verse structure.

3. **V. 35 -- shakhan / epeskiazen:** The MT's shakhan ("dwelt/tabernacled") is rendered in the LXX as epeskiazen ("overshadowed") -- a notably different theological nuance. The MT emphasizes indwelling (the cloud takes up residence), while the LXX emphasizes covering/overshadowing (the cloud casts its shadow over the tent). Both are valid readings of the Hebrew, but "dwelt" preserves the shakhan-mishkan wordplay that is the theological cornerstone of the tabernacle concept. Luke 1:35 will later echo the LXX's episkiasei ("will overshadow") in the annunciation narrative. SOPHIA follows the MT.

4. **V. 38 -- "House of Israel" / "all Israel":** The MT's kol beit Yisra'el ("all the house of Israel") is rendered in the LXX as pantos Israel ("all Israel"), dropping the "house" (beit/oikos) metaphor. SOPHIA preserves "the house of Israel" from the MT, as this is the final phrase of the book of Exodus and its designation of Israel as a unified household under YHWH's roof is theologically significant -- the people who dwell in the house of YHWH see YHWH's presence dwelling in the tabernacle.

5. **The divine name:** The LXX renders the tetragrammaton as kyrios ("Lord") throughout. SOPHIA transliterates YHWH per binding conventions.

6. **V. 34 -- male' / eplesthe:** The MT's male' ("filled," active/stative Qal) is rendered by the LXX as eplesthe ("was filled," aorist passive). The MT presents YHWH's glory as the active agent that fills; the LXX presents the tabernacle as the passive recipient that is filled. SOPHIA follows the MT's active construction: "the glory of YHWH filled the tabernacle."

---

## Consensus Notes

This chapter exhibited the highest agreement rate of any chapter in the final construction block (Exodus 35-40), owing to its highly formulaic structure and the gravitas of the closing theophany. The eight-fold repetition of "as YHWH commanded Moses" in vv. 19-33 was rendered identically by all three translators, as was the cloud-and-glory language of vv. 34-35. The primary disagreements were terminological (parokhet, qodesh qodashim, shakhan) and aspectual (imperfect verbs in vv. 36-38), all of which have been addressed consistently throughout the forty-chapter translation project.

Exodus ends as it must: with YHWH filling the house he commanded to be built, dwelling among the people he liberated, and leading them by cloud and fire into an open future. The translation is complete.
